title: Quantum Causal Discovery via Amplitude Estimation of Kullback-Leibler Divergence
abstract: Causal discovery from observational data underpins applications in finance, climate modeling, and machine learning. Constraint-based causal discovery reduces structure learning to a sequence of conditional independence (CI) tests, where each test decides independence by estimating conditional mutual information $I(X;Y \mid Z)$ to additive precision $τ$ and thresholding against it. Classically this requires $Θ(1/τ^{2})$ samples per test, a cost that dominates in the high-precision regime typical of weak dependencies. We present QKLA (Quantum Kullback--Leibler Amplitude estimation), a quantum algorithm that encodes a clipped log-density ratio as a bounded amplitude and applies amplitude estimation to recover the KL divergence. Given coherent oracle access to the joint distribution, QKLA achieves a quadratic precision improvement, needing only $\mathcal{O}((L/τ)\log(1/δ))$ queries, where $L$ is the log-ratio clip bound. Embedded in the PC algorithm, this compounds to an $\widetildeΩ(1/(Lτ))$ reduction in total queries for the full causal discovery procedure. We validate the theory in three experiments. A gate-level state-vector simulation of the full QKLA circuit confirms the predicted $\mathcal{O}(1/M)$ error decay. Across $K=20$ random binary distributions, classical and quantum error scalings match theory to slope accuracy $\pm 0.005$. On two benchmark networks (\textsc{Asia}, 8 nodes; \textsc{Synthetic-12}, 12 nodes), quantum PC matches classical skeleton-recovery F1 while using $2.5$--$3.0\times$ fewer oracle queries at $τ= 5\cdot 10^{-3}$ bits and up to $12\times$ fewer at $τ= 10^{-3}$ bits.
summary: This paper introduces QKLA, a quantum algorithm designed to accelerate causal discovery by efficiently estimating Kullback-Leibler divergence. By leveraging Quantum Amplitude Estimation, the algorithm achieves a quadratic speedup in precision compared to classical sampling methods. Simulations and benchmarks on networks like ASIA and SYNTHETIC-12 demonstrate that the quantum approach can use significantly fewer oracle queries to achieve the same structural accuracy. This is particularly impactful in high-precision regimes where detecting weak dependencies is critical.
main_problem: The computational bottleneck in constraint-based causal discovery is the high cost of conditional independence tests when high precision is required to detect weak dependencies.
main_result: The authors present QKLA, a quantum algorithm that achieves a quadratic speedup in precision, reducing query complexity from O(1/tau^2) to O(1/tau) and significantly reducing total queries for the PC algorithm.
method: The QKLA algorithm uses Quantum Amplitude Estimation (QAE) to encode a clipped log-density ratio as a bounded amplitude, combined with Grover iterations and the inverse Quantum Fourier Transform.
model_or_system: The algorithm operates in a quantum oracle model involving a preparation oracle for joint distributions and a reversible arithmetic oracle for computing clipped log-density ratios.
key_observables: Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence, Conditional Mutual Information (CMI), and F1 score for network skeleton recovery.
important_parameters: Target additive precision (tau), clip bound (L), failure probability (delta), and the high-precision regime where tau is small (10^-3 to 10^-2 bits).
main_assumptions: The efficiency assumes efficient quantum oracles for distribution preparation and log-ratio computation; the use of a clip bound L introduces a small bias on extreme distributions.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the phase-register output distribution and error scaling on log-log plots; Figure 2 illustrates classical vs. quantum query scaling and identifies the crossover point for precision advantage.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the causal discovery problem, presents the QKLA and QCMIE algorithms, provides a theoretical complexity analysis, validates the theory via gate-level simulations, and benchmarks performance on standard causal networks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Experiment 1: gate-level state-vector simulation of the QKLA circuit on the fixed target pxy = [[0.4, 0.1], [0.1, 0.4]], L = 2, narith = 6. (a) Phase-register output distribution at t = 5 (M = 2t = 32): two peaks at the canonical locations m⋆and M −m⋆, each carrying mass ≈0.395, sum ≈0.79 (above the 8/π2 ≈0.811 QAE guarantee only at worst-case phase alignment; here the phase is closer to that worst case so we land slightly below it). (b) 80th-percentile absolute KL error vs M, with the leading-order BHMT reference line 2Lπ/M (from |˜a −a| ≤π/M after rescaling by 2L). Log-log slope is −1.38 (least-squares fit), steeper than the −1 rate predicted by this leading-order bound; the 4Lπ/M...

Fig. 2. Experiment 2: classical vs quantum query scaling, averaged across K = 20 random binary 2 × 2 joint distributions with MI in [0.03, 0.32] bits. (A) 90th-percentile error vs total oracle queries. Fitted slopes: classical −0.500 ± 0.004, quantum −1.001 ± 0.005. Dotted lines are the theoretical references ∝n−1/2 and ∝M−1. (B) Minimum queries needed for 90th-percentile error ≤τ, plotted against 1/τ. Classical tracks τ −2; quantum tracks τ −1. Crossover near 1/τ ≈40; at 1/τ = 200 the quantum algorithm needs 4.2× fewer queries.

Fig. 3. Experiment 3: PC skeleton-recovery F1 versus total oracle queries on two benchmark networks. Classical PC uses the plug-in CMI estimator with n = ⌈2/τ 2⌉i.i.d. samples per test; quantum PC uses QCMIE with the empirically-sufficient M = ⌈2πL/τ⌉iterations per test (L = 3, nshots = 5), which is half the worst-case ⌈4πL/τ⌉of Theorem 6—justified by the measured slope −1.38 in Fig. 2(b). Error bars show ±1 s.d. over five trials. Dotted vertical lines mark the finest precision in the sweep (τ = 10−3); at that point, quantum needs 12.0× fewer queries on ASIA and 7.2× fewer on SYNTHETIC-12.
